title: States of matter — solids, liquids and gases explained by particles
examTip: Remember: during a change of state the temperature stays CONSTANT — energy is breaking bonds, not raising kinetic energy. Examiners love this.
introTips: Think about why steam burns you worse than boiling water — the particle model explains this.
The difference between a solid and a gas is about 10 million times less density — all because of how spread out the particles are.
Understanding this topic unlocks pressure, density, and latent heat — it's the foundation of the whole unit.
intro: Why can you squash a gas but not a liquid? Why does ice feel solid but water pour freely, even though both are the same substance? The answer lies in the arrangement and energy of the particles inside them. The particle model is one of the most powerful ideas in physics — it explains the behaviour of all matter using one simple principle: everything is made of tiny particles in constant motion, and the differences between solids, liquids and gases come down to how those particles are arranged and how much energy they have.

keyPoints.heading: The three states of matter
keyPoints.content: Everything around you exists as a solid, liquid or gas — and the particle model explains why each state behaves the way it does. Solid: particles are closely packed together in a regular lattice arrangement. They vibrate about fixed positions but cannot move past each other. There are strong forces of attraction between particles. Solids have a fixed shape and fixed volume, and are virtually incompressible. Liquid: particles are close together but not in a regular arrangement. They can move past each other freely. Forces between particles are weaker than in a solid. Liquids have a fixed volume but take the shape of their container. Like solids, they are virtually incompressible — you cannot squeeze the particles any closer together. Gas: particles are far apart and move quickly in random directions. Forces between particles are negligible (essentially zero). Gases have no fixed shape or volume and are highly compressible — there is a lot of empty space between particles that can be reduced. Changes of state involve changing the arrangement and energy of particles — the particles themselves are NOT created or destroyed.
writeBullets: Solid — particles in regular lattice, vibrate about fixed positions, strong forces, fixed shape and volume
Liquid — particles close but not in regular arrangement, can move past each other, weaker forces, fixed volume
Gas — particles far apart, random rapid motion, negligible forces, no fixed shape or volume
Solids and liquids are virtually incompressible; gases are highly compressible
Changes of state change particle arrangement and energy — particles are NOT destroyed

keyTerms.def: A regular, repeating arrangement of particles in a solid
Cannot be compressed — volume stays constant under pressure
Attractive forces between particles that hold them together

keyPoints.memTip: SLiG: Solid = Lattice, Liquid = loose, Gas = gone (far apart). Each letter steps up in freedom.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often say 'particles in a solid don't move' — they DO vibrate; they just can't change position. Correct this firmly.

keyPoints.heading: Internal energy and the kinetic theory model
keyPoints.content: The internal energy of a substance is the total kinetic and potential energy stored by all its particles. This is not the same as temperature — it depends on how many particles there are AND how much energy each one has. When you heat a substance, you increase its internal energy. This can happen in two ways: (a) Temperature rises: particles move faster — their kinetic energy increases. This is what happens most of the time. (b) Change of state occurs: particles gain potential energy as bonds between them break or weaken. During this process, temperature stays CONSTANT even though energy is still being added. The kinetic theory model states: - All matter is made of particles in constant random motion - The higher the temperature, the greater the average kinetic energy of the particles - Temperature (in Kelvin) is proportional to the average kinetic energy of the particles This model explains why gases exert pressure (particles hit walls), why diffusion happens (particles spread out randomly), and why substances expand when heated (particles move faster and push further apart).
writeBullets: Internal energy — total kinetic and potential energy of ALL particles in a system
Heating increases internal energy — either raises temperature OR causes change of state
Temperature rise — particles gain kinetic energy (move faster)
Change of state — particles gain potential energy as bonds break; temperature stays constant
Kinetic theory — all particles in constant motion; higher temperature = greater average kinetic energy
Kelvin temperature is directly proportional to average kinetic energy of particles

keyTerms.def: The total kinetic and potential energy stored by all particles in a system
Energy due to motion — particles moving faster have more kinetic energy
Energy stored in the bonds/forces between particles

keyPoints.memTip: Two effects of heating: MOVE faster (kinetic energy ↑) or MOVE apart (potential energy ↑, bonds break). Temperature only rises for the first one.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The key exam point: temperature constant during state change. Draw a heating curve and mark the flat sections clearly.

keyPoints.heading: Changes of state
keyPoints.content: A change of state occurs when a substance changes from one state to another. All changes of state are reversible — the same substance, just rearranged. Melting (solid → liquid): particles gain energy, bonds weaken, particles begin to move past each other. Occurs at the melting point. Freezing (liquid → solid): particles lose energy, slow down, and arrange themselves into a lattice. Occurs at the same temperature as melting point. Evaporation (liquid → gas): happens at the surface at ANY temperature. Faster-moving particles escape from the surface. This is why wet washing dries even when cold. Boiling (liquid → gas): happens throughout the liquid at a specific temperature (boiling point). Bubbles of vapour form throughout the liquid. Condensation (gas → liquid): particles lose energy, slow down, and bonds reform. Happens when gas cools. Sublimation (solid → gas directly): e.g., solid carbon dioxide ('dry ice') sublimates at room temperature. CRUCIAL POINT: Temperature stays constant during any change of state. Energy is being used to break intermolecular bonds, not to increase kinetic energy.
writeBullets: Melting — solid → liquid, particles gain energy and break free from lattice positions
Freezing — liquid → solid, particles lose energy and form a lattice
Evaporation — liquid → gas at any temperature, from the surface only
Boiling — liquid → gas at boiling point, throughout the liquid
Condensation — gas → liquid, particles lose energy and bonds reform
Sublimation — solid → gas directly (e.g., dry ice CO₂)

keyTerms.def: The temperature at which a substance changes from solid to liquid (same as freezing point)
The specific temperature at which a liquid boils throughout its volume
A change of state directly from solid to gas without passing through liquid

keyPoints.memTip: MFECBS — Melt, Freeze, Evaporate, Condense, Boil, Sublime. Or think of a heating curve: slope-flat-slope-flat.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Evaporation vs boiling is a common exam question — make sure you can explain the difference clearly.

keyPoints.heading: Limitations of the particle model
keyPoints.content: The simple particle model treats particles as identical, solid, featureless spheres. This is a useful simplification — but it is a model, not reality. What the model gets right: - Explains density differences between states (particles far apart in gas → low density) - Explains gas pressure (particles colliding with container walls) - Explains diffusion (particles move randomly and spread out) - Explains why substances expand when heated (particles move faster, push further apart) - Explains changes of state qualitatively Where the model is limited: - Real atoms have internal structure: electrons, protons, neutrons (the model gives no information about this) - Interparticle forces are complex electrical interactions — not simply 'strong' or 'weak' - The model does not explain the different properties of different materials (why gold is so dense, why diamond is so hard) - It does not account for quantum effects that become important at very small scales For GCSE purposes, the particle model is both powerful and sufficient — it accurately predicts and explains a huge range of physical phenomena. Acknowledging its limitations is itself an important scientific skill.
writeBullets: Particle model treats particles as identical solid spheres — a useful simplification
Model explains: density, gas pressure, diffusion, expansion, changes of state
Limitation — ignores internal structure of atoms (electrons, protons, neutrons)
Limitation — interparticle forces are complex electrical interactions, not simple attractions
Limitation — cannot explain differences in properties between different materials
Scientific models are powerful tools even when they have limitations

keyTerms.def: A simplified representation of reality used to explain and predict observations
The spreading out of particles from high to low concentration due to random motion

keyPoints.memTip: All models are wrong, some are useful — the particle model is very useful at GCSE even though it's simplified.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Higher-tier students may be asked to evaluate the particle model — practise explaining both its successes and its limits.

tables.title: Comparing the three states of matter

0.question: Which statement correctly describes particles in a liquid?
options: Particles are close together and can move past each other, giving a fixed volume but no fixed shape
Particles are in a regular lattice and vibrate about fixed positions
Particles are far apart and move rapidly in random directions
Particles are fixed in position but can vibrate with large amplitude
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Liquids have particles close together (fixed volume, virtually incompressible) but free to move past each other (no fixed shape).
0.wrongFeedback: Think carefully about the three states. Liquids have fixed volume (particles are still close together) but no fixed shape (particles can flow past each other). The regular lattice is the solid; the far-apart random motion is the gas.

1.question: A substance is being heated at its melting point. What is happening to its temperature and internal energy?
options: Temperature stays constant; internal energy increases (particles gain potential energy as bonds break)
Temperature stays constant; internal energy stays constant
1.correctFeedback: Perfect. At a change of state, temperature is constant — but energy is still going in, increasing internal energy by breaking intermolecular bonds (potential energy increases).
1.wrongFeedback: Remember: heating always increases internal energy. But at a change of state, that extra energy goes into breaking bonds (potential energy), not speeding particles up (kinetic energy). So temperature stays constant.

2.question: What is the difference between evaporation and boiling?
options: Evaporation happens at the surface at any temperature; boiling happens throughout the liquid at a specific temperature
Evaporation only happens at the boiling point; boiling can happen at any temperature
Evaporation is a gas turning to liquid; boiling is a liquid turning to gas
There is no difference — they are the same process
2.correctFeedback: Spot on. Evaporation is a surface phenomenon at any temperature (faster particles escape). Boiling is throughout the liquid at a fixed boiling point.
2.wrongFeedback: Don't confuse these two. Evaporation: surface only, any temperature — this is why wet clothes dry even when cold. Boiling: throughout the liquid, only at the specific boiling point.

commonMistakes: Saying particles in a solid 'don't move' — they DO vibrate about fixed positions; they just cannot change their position in the lattice.
Thinking temperature rises during a change of state — it stays constant because energy is breaking bonds, not increasing kinetic energy.
Confusing evaporation and boiling — evaporation is from the surface at any temperature; boiling is throughout the liquid at the boiling point.
Saying particles are 'destroyed' or 'lost' during a change of state — the same particles are there, just rearranged with different energy.
Mixing up kinetic and potential energy during heating — temperature change = kinetic energy change; state change = potential energy change.
examTips: If asked what happens to temperature during a change of state: it stays constant. Explain that energy goes into breaking bonds (potential energy increases), not into speeding up particles.
When comparing states, always mention particle arrangement, movement, forces, and whether the substance has fixed shape/volume.
Distinguish clearly between evaporation (surface, any temperature) and boiling (throughout liquid, fixed boiling point) — this distinction comes up regularly.
revisionCardBullets: Solid: regular lattice, vibrate about fixed positions, strong forces, fixed shape and volume
Liquid: close particles, irregular arrangement, can move past each other, fixed volume only
Gas: far apart particles, rapid random motion, negligible forces, no fixed shape or volume
Gases are highly compressible; solids and liquids are virtually incompressible
Internal energy = total kinetic + potential energy of all particles
Heating can raise temperature (kinetic energy ↑) OR cause state change (potential energy ↑)
During a change of state: temperature stays CONSTANT
Evaporation: surface only, any temperature; Boiling: throughout liquid, at boiling point
Melting: bonds weaken, particles move past each other; Freezing: reverse
Sublimation: solid → gas directly (e.g., dry ice)
Particle model limitation: treats particles as simple spheres — ignores internal atomic structure
Particle model successfully explains: density differences, gas pressure, diffusion, expansion
